title: Dan Carlin, A Hardcore Tolucan
author: Gino Renzulli
excerpt: Dan Carlin, creator of Hardcore History, grew up right here in Toluca Lake — and he had a lot to say about the neighborhood he still calls his foundational place.

blocks.content:
Dan Carlin is a name that will be familiar to many of you as the creator of one of the most widely listened to podcasts in America, Hardcore History. A smaller fraction of you, if you have lived in Toluca Lake since the 1970s, may have actually known Dan or the Carlin family personally. It recently came to my attention that Carlin, a man whose work I greatly admire and whose voice I have listened to for over 100 hours, had lived in Toluca Lake and in fact grew up with some of my closest family friends.

Upon learning this, I knew I had to reach out to Carlin, and thankfully, Dan could not have been more receptive. I was thrilled to receive a response, and even more excited that Carlin was eager to talk and fondly remembered our mutual friends. Carlin graciously offered his time for a lengthy and wide-ranging interview over the phone with The Tolucan Times. Carlin was phenomenally kind, open, and a model interviewee, which should come as no surprise considering he has made a living as an eloquent communicator.

Carlin started his career in journalism originally working at KJH-TV, which would later become KCAL. "I got a coffee-getter, intern-runner, gofer type job at channel 9." Right on cue, Dan had a history tidbit to share about channel 9's history. "This is how old [KJH-TV's] manager was. It was an RKO General station. I think RKO General was a tire company in the Depression, and they bought a bunch of media outlets, and by the time I worked there, they were still hobbling along with something they didn't even know why they still owned anymore. I'm not even sure they still sold tires at that point. And while I was there, it got bought by Disney." Dan's story checks out, a tire company called General Tire owned channel 9 in Los Angeles from 1951 until 1989. He continued to work there under Disney for a while. "From there I got a job – I didn't want one – but the guys at KABC were pushing me saying: 'You know, you'll die at this job if you don't keep moving up the ladder.' They were encouraging me to be a reporter. And I think to just shut them up, I sent out some tapes because nobody was getting hired who were sending out tapes, so I didn't think it was much of a risk. I got hired in Eugene, Oregon. So I moved up here, became a TV reporter, and moved over to talk radio."

Carlin's career in broadcast journalism clearly served as a foundation for his future career in podcasting, but I wondered too if his upbringing in Toluca Lake, a neighborhood known for its many entertainers, influenced his gravitation towards entertaining others. "It's funny because I often think back to Toluca Lake, and I'll tell my wife, who's from Oregon, stories about [Toluca Lake] and it's different now than it was then. When I think of what the parents of all of the kids that I knew did, it was two general areas of employment: Either the entertainment industry – in front of behind the camera – or aerospace." Unsurprisingly, again, Carlin was ready to teach me a bit of history that I didn't know, but this time about our neighborhood. "There were a ton of people that worked Lockheed, McDonnell, Douglas, you know, all of those kinds of firms. My next-door neighbor's dad was an Air Force colonel who worked for Lockheed, and there were a lot of those guys. That seemed to be like that because Lockheed was huge and it probably was not so huge by your time, but this was the Cold War, you know? I moved to Toluca Lake late 1960s, and it was a huge industry in the Valley."

Today, I doubt many of us think of Toluca Lake as a neighborhood that caters specifically to military contractors, but I'm sure there are still a few in our midst. Carlin's family belonged to the other of the two major industries at the time in the area: entertainment. His mother, Lynn Carlin, was an actress in the 70s and 80s when Dan was growing up. She has over 60 on-screen credits, including a starring role alongside Toluca Lake's late, great honorary sheriff (and Dan Carlin's first little league coach) Joe Campanella in the 1974 film Terror on the 40th Floor. Carlin has been an undeniable trailblazer in the world of podcasting having first entered the sphere in 2005, long before the podcasting boom with his show Hardcore History that now receives millions of downloads per episode. "When we started in 2005, we started the very month that Apple started supporting podcasts. So we really didn't think anybody knew what [podcasting] was going to be. Most of the people doing this were like nerds in their dorm rooms in college just reading stuff into a microphone or whatever. I mean, it was just so eclectic and weird and strange that I don't think any of us really grasp what any of this was."

Carlin told me that he has always been fond of his time in Toluca Lake, where he lived for the first twelve years of his life in houses on both Navajo and Rose. "That's my foundational place," he said of our neighborhood and went on to share some charming stories from that time in Toluca Lake's history. "Did anybody ever mention the candy ladies to you?" Carlin asked me. "There were two – and I always think of them as old women but I thought about it the other day and they probably were my age – but these women were the candy ladies. If you would knock on their door and you were a child, they didn't even ask anything they just came to the door with a whole tureen of candy. They would come any hour, day or night and do this. And every kid, of course, in the neighborhood knew about this. It was a landmark."

Carlin remarked too how the times have changed and the thought of sending one's kids to the mysterious candy ladies' house would be almost unthinkable to many parents today. On the topic of the Toluca Lake that he grew up in, Carlin told me a couple of his favorite old spots. "One place that I used to go to that my dad would roll his eyes when I made him go there was called Saint George Smorgasbord. It had like a knightly theme, Knights of the Round Table kind of thing. But it was a smorgasbord, so it had every kind of food you can imagine. It would have been like an upscale version of Golden Corral or something." Another old favorite Carlin mentioned that I'm sure many readers will recall: The Hot Dog Show, which was a Toluca Lake staple for 62 years before it closed its doors in 2011.

It was an incredible honor and pleasure to speak with Dan, and I want to leave you all with some of his words about Toluca Lake and what it has meant to him throughout his life. "It's so funny that just in my little town here where I live in Eugene, Oregon, it just reminds me of how I felt in Toluca Lake when I was a kid. Just because the big, wide world of Los Angeles really wasn't a part of my life, you know? What was a part of my life was from Riverside Drive to Lakeside Golf Course. That little square area that was my whole world, and it was nice. It was green; it was safe, and that's kind of how this community where I live right now is. It reminds me a little of how my mom always said I was looking to get back to Toluca Lake ever since we left."

title: The Honorary Mayor Is In!
author: Staff
excerpt: Meet the honorary mayor of Toluca Lake and learn about the beloved local tradition that has brought our community together for decades.

blocks.content:
There are always a handful of names that get thrown around when we talk about the legendary members of our community. A few of them sang songs, and some acted in those fancy Hollywood moving pictures. Yet none of them earned the prestigious title of "Honorary Mayor." That belongs to one man and one man only… Fritz Coleman.

Although Fritz is most well known for his nearly 40-year run as the weatherman on Channel 4, his career at NBC may not have ever happened if it wasn't for his passion for stand-up comedy. "I came out here in 1980, to pursue a career in stand-up. In 1982, I was working [as a performer] at The Comedy Store. My friend, at the time, who worked at Channel 4, brought his boss and his boss's wife to see me do a show on a Friday night," Fritz explained. He continued, "I had talked on stage about doing weather in the Navy, I was in the Navy for 4 years and did armed forces radio and television. I was forced to do the weather against my will."

Although Fritz didn't know much of anything about the weather, Channel 4 saw him as a perfect fit. Fritz recalled the Channel 4 boss saying "It's perfect because there's no weather in California, this will work out great." A stand-up background was more than ideal, as he pointed out. "When you're doing the weather, no one is helping you out… You would figure out the weather story, makeup four or five bullet points, and conversate the audience through that story." Fritz ended up working at Channel 4 until his retirement in 2020, only 2 weeks shy of his 40th anniversary at NBC.

Through all his years on air, Fritz continued to do comedy as an outlet to talk about something other than the never-ending supply of vitamin D we get here in Southern California. The Honorary Mayor explained, "I do single-topic monologues, I've been doing it for about 30 years." His first show was called 'It's Me, Dad,' which was about life as a divorced father. His second show, 'The Reception' covered the topic of being divorced as well. Next came 'Tonight at 11' which gave insight into his time on the news. The fourth installment of his single topic monologues, 'Defying Gravity,' was a show about getting older. His current show, which Fritz titled 'Unassisted Living,' is about getting really old.

"Unassisted Living," which has been playing once a month at The El Portal Theatre, has been extended for a third time. Come October, the show will have been running for a year. For those of you who want to see it, the next performance is Sunday, July 21st at 3 pm. Seeing as how this is The Tolucan Times, I had to do my journalistic duty for our community. I had to know what it really means to be our Honorary Mayor. "I've been the Honorary Mayor for 28 years. I have no political power, I can't even get a parking ticket fixed in this town," Fritz quipped. "But I love it. It's public relations for this community that I love very much."

Having been a part of the community for so long, Fritz was truly excited to have The Tolucan Times return this past year. With a lifetime of experience in news and media, he sees the importance of a local news source. He says, "There is a school of thought that news is going to become hyper-local. These papers are going to be more valuable to people's lives than something like the LA Times… I think this type of paper is going to be the future." Like many of us, he has a story of his first time seeing the actual Toluca Lake. "At first I thought the lake was just a legend, like the Loch Ness Monster, only rich people could get back there. Then I got a couple of friends, through my acquaintances in Toluca Lake, invited me back there. It's a beautiful spot, with these spectacular homes," he shared. When I asked him where in Toluca Lake he eats, he swiftly responded "Breakfast at Bob's Big Boy, dinner at Paty's." I don't think there is a more Toluca Lake response than that. Follow Fritz on Instagram: @RealFritzColeman.

title: 818 Files: Hello Girls in a Flood
author: Jeremy Oberstein
excerpt: The Night the Dam Gave Way

blocks.content:
Just before 1:30 a.m. on March 12, 1928, Louise Gipe got the alert of what would end up being the deadliest man-made disaster in California history.

The newly built St. Francis Dam failed. Billions of gallons of water were already pouring out of San Francisquito Canyon and racing toward the coast, tearing through what is now Santa Clarita and Castaic, killing more than 400 people as it followed the Santa Clara River toward the sea.

Gipe was the overnight telephone operator in nearby Santa Paula. In an instant, her shift changed from passing time amid the quiet hum of midnight to something closer to what we would today consider emergency dispatch. She had no formal training for this but she knew exactly what had to be done.

Years earlier, the arid and rapidly growing city of Los Angeles looked for ways to engineer around its most basic problem. William Mulholland, the Chief Engineer of what was then called the Bureau of Water Works and Supply, had already secured the Owens Valley as a distant source of water, but that solution came with conflict, cost and violence. City leaders wanted something closer, calmer and easier to control.

Mulholland identified San Francisquito Canyon, about 45 miles north of downtown Los Angeles, as an ideal site. The uninhabited canyon near Castaic Lake narrowed downstream and widened upstream, a natural configuration that allowed for a large reservoir behind a comparatively modest dam. On paper, it looked efficient.

But Mulholland had doubts. In 1911, he warned city officials about the “exceedingly rough” geological conditions in the canyon. The project moved forward anyway, with Mulholland leading on-the-ground efforts.

Thousands of workers spent 16 months constructing the 180-foot-tall, 600-foot-wide dam that held 13 billion gallons of Owens Valley water for the city of Los Angeles. The reservoir was gradually filled and reached capacity on March 7, 1928. Almost immediately, engineers noticed small leaks and muddy seepage, signs that water was distressingly moving through the foundation.

On the night of March 12, Ace Hopewell, a carpenter, rode his motorcycle past the dam just before midnight when he heard a rumbling sound, like rocks rolling down a hillside. He stopped, lit a cigarette and checked the hillside, where landslides were not uncommon. The sound faded. Hopewell rode on, the last person to see the dam intact.

Minutes later, fissures became fractures. The dam collapsed completely and the canyon filled with moving water, swallowing everything in its path.

The first formal warning went out at 1:20 a.m. By then, the flood had already traveled roughly 18 miles and crested more than 40 feet, wiping out workers’ camps, killing hundreds.

Faster Than the Flood

When the alert reached her, Louise Gipe didn’t hesitate.

She relayed the warning immediately to police officers Thornton Edwards and Stanley Baker, to city officials and then to homes in the low-lying parts of Santa Paula. The phones became lifelines. For a brief and critical stretch of time, information moved faster than water.

Edwards and Baker mounted their motorcycles and rode through the night, pounding on doors and shouting warnings to families who had gone to bed unaware of the impending catastrophe. Their ride ended at 3:05 a.m., when a 30-foot wall of water tore through Santa Paula on its way toward Ventura and the ocean.

While the motorcycles ran, Louise Gipe stayed at the switchboard.

So did Reicel Jones and Althea Marks, telephone operators downriver in nearby Saticoy. None of them knew how high the water would be by the time it reached their towns. They only knew it had been more than 100 feet high when it left the dam and that their neighbors had to be warned while the lines still worked.

They kept calling.

“Courageous telephone operators… stayed at their perilous posts, calling frantically ahead, trying to outrace the flood by telephone,” historian Cecilia Rasmussen later wrote.

Louise became the most storied of them because Santa Paula was the largest town in the flood’s path and her quick hands and faster thinking saved lives. For nearly six hours, water poured from the top of the dam west toward the Pacific Ocean.

By morning, entire families along the river corridor were gone. Half of the students from Saugus Elementary School were never heard from again. Some victims were found miles inland or decades later. Others were never recovered. But countless others were saved thanks to the operators who became known as the “Hello Girls.”

“Only one thing traveled faster than the floodwaters,” said historian John Nichols, “and that was (their) emergency calls.”

The Cost and the Reckoning

A reckoning followed. Mulholland testified that he had taken “all the care that prudence suggested,” but a jury concluded the failure stemmed from “an error in engineering judgment in determining the foundation,” placing responsibility squarely on the city’s chief engineer. Mulholland stepped down, retreated from public life and died in 1935.

California rewrote its dam safety laws. Engineering oversight was professionalized after officials said such construction “should never be left to the sole judgment of one man, no matter how eminent … for no one is free from error.” Years later, officials found that the dam’s eastern edge sat on an ancient landslide that shifted and plowed into the structure “like a bulldozer blade.”

At that point, Louise Gipe’s job was done. For a few, brief hours, the unlikeliest of heroes answered the call and reacted with the kind of grace under fire that comes when we least expect it.

“Many people are alive today because of heroic acts that were performed on the night of March 12, 1928,” historian Nichols said during a 75th anniversary commemoration.

“Many here survived and went on to have children, grandchildren and great grandchildren. Entire family lines could have been snuffed out without the acts of heroism we honor today.”

Jeremy Oberstein is a proud Valley resident. Let him know your favorite Valley history story at

jeremy@10thstcomms.com

title: Local Fun: Warner Bros. Studio Tour
author: Francesca Domenica
excerpt: One of Hollywood's most iconic studios sits just down the road. We took the tour — here's everything you need to know before you go.

blocks.content:
It's rare for Angelenos to venture to local tourist spots. Whether it's the avoid-at-all-costs Hollywood Boulevard or something more accessible and enjoyable like The Getty Museum, we tend to only visit these locales when family is in town or there's a spark of adventure on a day off.

While it might feel silly engaging in your own town's tourism, there's one particular outing that is well worth your time and it's just a light breeze away from Toluca Lake! The Warner Brothers Studio Tour offered daily on their Burbank lot, is an immersive, entertaining, and welcoming experience.

Many of you reading this might be members of the entertainment industry thinking, "Oh, Tolucan Times Writer, I've worked on that lot before, I don't need a tour!" Fair enough, but what you may not know is that this tour offers a charming, educational look back at Hollywood history allowing visitors to take a leisurely stroll down cinema's memory lane. Giving not only a look at sets and props, The WB Tour, led by a thoroughly knowledgeable guide, provides an enthusiastic lesson in cinematic history that not only enlivens the magic of filmmaking but inspires a craving for both creating and consuming film and television.

In the span of two hours, you are taken in small groups on a comfortable tour cart, rolling past massive soundstages, learning where great films like "Casablanca" (1942), "My Fair Lady" (1964), and beloved series "Friends" and "ER" were filmed, feeling an immediate sense of history on this hallowed ground.

One major portion of the studio lot, a city square and small neighborhood, most notably featured in "Gilmore Girls," transports you from Burbank straight to the fictional town of Stars Hollow, Connecticut. By allowing tour goers to exit the cart and walk around, enter the houses, take photos in the gazebo, you're swept up in the illusion of this all encompassing set. The rolling Valley hills slip away feeling as though you're in an idyllic New England small town.

For those not so interested in that particular TV-fare, or perhaps for those who haven't been on this tour since their massive update in 2021, a number of interactive exhibits are included in your excursion, namely a large space dedicated to the DC Comics Universe and the Harry Potter franchise.

Last but certainly not least are a pair of dazzling gift shops, one for the Friends fanatics (which includes a free photo-op in the real "Central Perk" set) and the other a WB wide store chock-full of exclusive merchandise.

As for this writer's personal experience of the tour, I can say I had nothing short of a blast! On a sunny day back in May I, along with a friend and fellow TV enthusiast, enjoyed walking the streets of Stars Hollow. We peered around recognizable homes, the cafe from "La La Land" (2017), traveled down "New York Street" up to the "Friends" fountain, walked the halls of a meticulously decorated high school set currently being used for the series "All-American," finishing up at an exhibit where we gazed at Old Hollywood wardrobe, iconic props, screenplays, and coveted award statuettes.

There is so much Hollywood history surrounding us here in Toluca Lake, whether it's the quiet notes of the past alongside our adored tinseltown streets or something as forward and dynamic as this Warner Brothers Tour, what a privilege to be able to be so immersed in it all!

title: The Man, The myth, Your Neighbor: Kato Kaelin
author: Gino Renzulli
excerpt: Never Has a Man Done So Little To Be Recognized By So Many

blocks.content:
Recently, I had the pleasure of spending nearly three hours getting to know Kato Kaelin. Some readers, especially those born after 1990, may not be immediately familiar with Kato, while readers over the age of 35 will likely have no trouble recalling America’s most famous guest house dweller. Part of my desire to interview Kato stemmed from getting to know him through his online presence and discovering what a light-hearted, entertaining guy he is. I immediately recognized the unfortunate irony that such a fun-loving person should be so inextricably attached to such a horrific event.

As such, I feel almost guilty even providing the basic context for Kato Kaelin’s fame. Kato Kaelin served as a witness in the O.J. Simpson murder trial in 1995, an event that became one of the largest media spectacles this country has ever seen. At the time of the crime, Kato was living in Simpson’s guest house. Almost overnight, he became a household name, and his memorable name, look, and mythos as the man in the back house became part of American cultural lore.

But that’s not who Kato wanted to be when he moved to Los Angeles in 1984, and that’s not how I’d like Tolucan readers to know him. Kato is a jovial, energetic, almost impish man who, in a more just world, would be more famous than he is now and for better reasons. Let’s get to know Kato Kaelin, who has been a Toluca Lake neighbor for over 20 years, for who he is and not for something that just so happened to involve him more than 30 years ago.

Kato is a multifaceted entertainer. An actor first, he has also spent decades hosting radio shows dating back to his college days in Wisconsin. For years, he emceed for Wizard World, the largest traveling comic convention, working all around the country in the process. Today, he hosts a true crime podcast called One Degree of Scandalous with his co-host Tom Zenner.

“We’ve been at it for three years, and I say the same thing all the time. After three years, we’ve hit the light at the end of the tunnel: advertisers. Finally, after persevering and working our asses off.”

Kato and Tom do their show from West Coast Creative Studio in Hollywood.
“It’s been such a challenge because we write the show, we get great guests, and now we’re getting a kind of a buzz, so it’s been great, but it’s taken some time.”

The podcast often features interviews with guests who, like Kato, have some connection to major crimes or scandals.

“One of our basic things we do, which is kind of cool, and our viewers love it, is we go to crime scenes where we take a tour, and then we have the drones. We did the Hillside Stranger with Detective Tom Lange and Bob Souza.”

Kato emanates enthusiasm as he tells me about his podcast. He has come to make the most of the hand he’s been dealt, though it took a long time for him to embrace his undeniable connection to true crime.
“Everything we do [on the podcast] has just been really cathartic for me because of the fact that it’s crime. I never wanted to embrace it. I hated being attached to the Simpson trial because I was this person of light and fun and happiness. And I was always reading for films. That was my life, to be an actor. And next thing you know, I’m thrust into the biggest trial of the century.”

“By no fault of my own, I became involved in this thing with the double edged sword of negativity and fame.”
“My wish of coming from Milwaukee, Wisconsin was: ‘Who doesn’t want to be famous? You kidding? Be on TV?’ But everything happened to me backwards. I became famous before people could say, ‘he has talent.’ Although, I had commercials, and I had my SAG card already.”

Growing up, Kato’s father owned two restaurants in Milwaukee along with a liquor distributorship. Although, as Kato was quick to point out, “My dad didn’t drink.” Both of his parents served in World War II, his father as a soldier and his mother as a nurse. He described his family as tight-knit and said he spoke with them often when he first moved to California.

“It was the loneliest time in my life because I didn’t drink or do drugs. Other people would get together to drink, so I would end up alone, and I hated it.”

At that time, Kato was taking acting classes under the late Scott Arthur Allen. It wasn’t all bad. Kato booked a national Coca-Cola commercial from his very first audition, an incredibly rare feat.
Kato had moved out to Los Angeles from his home state of Wisconsin around 1980 to pursue acting, but he was also an avid baseball player. He played baseball at his first college, the University of Wisconsin–Eau Claire. He tried out at Cal State Fullerton but admits the other guys were simply too big for him.
“I didn’t get whiskers until I was 38,” Kato said before repeating the phrase by yelling it into my phone that was recording our conversation to remove any doubt that it stood on the record.
Although baseball didn’t pan out professionally, he still loves watching and is an avid Wisconsin sports fan across the board.

As we chatted by the bar at the Hotel Amarano, where we met, Kato frequently waved at friends he knew and even engaged strangers in playful conversations, trying both to connect with them and make them laugh. That’s who Kato is. He’s a man of the people. When I told him that my mom actually worked on Ryan Murphy’s television dramatization of the Simpson trial, he insisted we call her, and they proceeded to talk for a while about some of the people he knew who worked on the show.

All is well for Kato, and he deserves it. He is starring in a new horror film titled A Soldier’s Descent, which is currently in post-production. He is happily married to his wife, Sofie Xue, who works at Compass Toluca Lake under Craig Strong’s team right here in Toluca Lake, and his podcast is getting more buzz than ever.
If you see Kato around the neighborhood, be sure to say hello. You won’t regret it!

title: L.A. In a Minute: Let's Get Into It
author: Gino Renzulli
excerpt: A fast-moving look at what's happening across Los Angeles — from culture and local politics to the stories shaping our neighborhood right now.

blocks.content:
Evan Lovett is a proud son of Los Angeles. Born and raised in the San Fernando Valley, Lovett has become something of a pop historian who studies the city he's spent his life in.

In the cluttered world of online media and "content," Lovett has carved out a niche for himself as an informative and accessible voice sharing the rich history of Los Angeles.

Lovett's series is called L.A. in a Minute, and if you use Instagram or TikTok, there is a good chance you have seen his videos. The concept is simple: Lovett is going to teach you something about Los Angeles in roughly a minute. He opens most reports with his sign-on: "What is up? This is your LA in a minute..." followed by a brief explanation of the video's topic and often bookended with the phrase "Let's get into it." Then, Evan unloads one of his many nuggets of history.

Since starting the series in 2020, Lovett has become a sensation. With hundreds of thousands of followers across multiple platforms, Evan has been formally honored by the City of Los Angeles and made numerous appearances on local news and radio stations. If you love history or the city of Los Angeles I highly recommend following Lovett. I was thrilled when I finally got the chance to speak to Evan about his well-earned fame and success.

Gino Renzulli: What was your career background before starting L.A. in a Minute?

Evan Lovett: I am a journalist at heart. I was the Sports Editor for the Daily Bruin at UCLA and was fortunate enough to land a gig for the L.A. Times coming out of school. That said, there was not enough upside for a young 21-year-old covering sports in Orange County (and returning back to DTLA to file stories) and I entered the private sector, first in PR before landing in online advertising for a company called The Vendare Group. I was fortunate to land in that space - it was really the beginning of the modern internet - and had a career lasting 20+ years.

GR: Did you always love history?

EL: Great question. I did not like history class in school, but I was always interested in historical footnotes, tidbits, and trivia. As a youngster, I was obsessed with sports and could rattle off baseball stats going back to Wee Willie Keeler and Ty Cobb, and through the days of Don Mattingly and Kirby Puckett. My parents also instilled in me a love of L.A., and they would take me around the Venice boardwalk, Griffith Park, South Los Angeles, and Boyle Heights and regale me with their personal histories, which unlocked a greater appreciation for the history of the city as well.

GR: How has your perception of LA changed since starting the show and researching the city?

EL: Oh my gosh. I've always loved Los Angeles and luckily I had a great appreciation of its diversity and rich culture growing up in the San Fernando Valley. In my homeroom in junior high, I had an Egyptian classmate, a Kenyan classmate, Armenian, Korean, Jewish — and even then I knew that this was special. It was so fun going to my friends' homes and trying their family's foods and just seeing things that were different. With that as a foundation, my appreciation of Los Angeles is always viewed through the prism of the richness of culture here in L.A. — despite the fact that people have always said, "L.A. has no history in culture." Well, the truth is, you just need to look for it.

GR: What's one piece of LA history every Angeleno should know about?

EL: The Pobladores that founded El Pueblo de Nuestra Señora la Reina de los Ángeles on September 4, 1781, was a reflection of the city today; 44 families, including Mexicans, Spaniards, Indigenous people, Africans, and Filipinos.

GR: Was there anyone doing content like this that inspired you?

EL: I am inspired by the people of L.A. every day. I owe a debt of gratitude to Huell Howser, Art Laboe, Vin Scully, and Jonathan Gold, as their approach to the 'regular' people, spots, and neighborhoods of Los Angeles were so authentic, deep-rooted, and genuine, that subconsciously I see reflections of their work in what I try to accomplish each day.

GR: How long would you say one video takes to complete from inception to the end of editing?

EL: From research to script to rewriting to driving to filming to editing... about eight hours per video. It's definitely a labor of love. My friend compares each video to "doing a term paper" and that sounds about right.

GR: Are you recognized on the streets often?

EL: Honestly, now I am recognized on a daily basis. It's really neat to know that I'm able to make an impact in this city. It's not about me. It's about uncovering the pride that people have in their neighborhood and culture, and my ability to shine a positive light on this city really resonates.

GR: Finally, what does the future hold for LA in a Minute?

EL: I would like to publish my book, 100 Stories of L.A. In a Minute, and also have the opportunity to turn L.A. in a Minute into a full-length show on a streaming service or a local network. In the meantime, the goal is to continue growing while shining a positive light on all of Los Angeles!

title: Backlot Business
author: Trevor Nystrom
excerpt: Why the Radford Studios Default Should Be a Wake-Up Call

blocks.content:
For nearly a century, Radford Studios has been more than just a collection of soundstages, it’s been the pulse of Hollywood. From the slapstick era of Mack Sennett to the “must-see” heights of Seinfeld, the local legendary lot on Radford Avenue wasn’t just a workplace, it was the identity of Studio City. When news broke that Hackman Capital Partners was ceding their keys to Goldman Sachs after a staggering $1.1 billion default, that once-healthy heartbeat began felt dangerously faint.

Mack Sennett Studios, Republic Pictures, CBS Radford— nicknamed “Hit City” after its success with Gunsmoke, Gilligan’s Island, and The Mary Tyler Moore Show —whatever you want to call it, the infamous backlot has been and will always be a cornerstone of Studio City. With recent programs like Parks and Recreation, Brooklyn Nine Nine, and The Conners occupying its sacred space so integral to the community that the suburb was officially named in its honor, it would be a deadly blow to our local economy. Originally founded in 1928 by Sennett, the “King of Comedy,” when he moved from Echo Park to Studio City, transforming a humble lettuce farm into a production titan. The lot eventually weathered bankruptcy to become Republic Pictures, the B-movie factory that launched the careers of John Wayne and Roy Rogers. Today, the 55-acre lot remains a ghost town of what it once was, a mere glimmer of Hollywood history forgotten by the times.

This collapse should serve as a “check-engine light” for the Los Angeles economy. Two years ago, we were promised a $1 billion modernization of a “New Hit City.” However, those renderings are nothing more than mere fantasies of disillusionment now. Ownership blames high interest rates and production droughts, but on Ventura Boulevard, the crisis is personal. A 63% occupancy rate isn’t just a statistic, it’s empty tables at restaurants and silence in nearby coffee shops. When revenue covers only 21% of debt, the community doesn’t just lose a developer, it loses an anchor.

There is a bitter irony in the fact that the very thing many neighbors feared, the massive, traffic-inducing expansion and parking problems, is now dead on arrival. Yet “victory” for the anti-development crowd feels hollow when the alternative is a bank-owned property in limbo. Goldman Sachs is in the business of assets, not artistry. While Hackman Capital was a developer with a specific vision for the future of Studio City, even if it seemed controversial to some, that prosperous vision is now gone. We’ve already seen reports of the lot becoming “decrepit” and “dangerous,” on February 6th, 2024, Marvel’s Wonder Man crew member, Juan Carlos “Spike” Osorio, died after falling from a catwalk, leading to a $36,000 fine against Disney for the deteriorated catwalk, with his family and union, IATSE, mourning the needless loss and advocation for greater set safety.

The Radford default should serve as a warning that Los Angeles can no longer take the entertainment industry for granted. When production flees, the dry cleaners, florists, and homeowners suffer first. To keep our industry alive, we need more than a landlord with deep pockets, we need a reckoning on how to keep Hollywood at home. If we fail, the only thing filmed on Radford Avenue will be a documentary about its glory days after it’s been torn down. The tragedy isn’t that the expansion failed, it’s the silence that has taken its place in Studio City.

title: The Magic Box
author: Johnny Campanella
excerpt: Brian Nowac’s Mobile LED Superstudios Empower Movie & TV Productions

blocks.content:
Bartending in this neighborhood for as long as I have affords me the privilege of meeting all sorts of interesting people, like Brian Nowac. Brian is the founder of Magicbox, a unique company that provides a revolutionary service to the movie, television, social media industries, and more.

Brian recently invited me to check out his amazing invention, Magicbox. I was intrigued and Brian is a great guy, so I drove down Hollywood Way on a warm, sunny January Saturday to take a gander.

Across the street from the Bob Hope Airport, in the Kino Flo parking lot, I exited my car and saw two large black trailers stationed there with the “Magicbox” logo painted in the ROY G. BIV color spectrum on their sides. One trailer was unfolded and the other one’s walls were packed up.

A mutual friend and neighborhood fixture, J.P. Kisob, greeted me (if you’re a local resident, you’ve seen J.P. somewhere in Toluca Lake). He escorted me into a luxurious production office trailer on loan from industry transportation giant, Galpin. I used to pick up box trucks at 5am from the Galpin lot in Hollywood many moons ago when I was a lowly P.A.! Instant street cred.

“They loan the trailer when Brian does his demonstrations,” J.P. told me. “Pretty swanky, man,” I replied. J.P. chuckled. We talked for a while as we waited for Brian to finish up with a group of producers inside the unfolded Magicbox.

J.P. works in Sales & Marketing for Magicbox. Prior to that, he was a Production Manager on commercials. He’s a professional and a genuine, supportive friend to Brian. I asked J.P. what exactly Magicbox is. He gave me broad-strokes. J.P. started by explaining one specific Magicbox application: shooting car process driving scenes. “That’s massive. There’s always a driving scene,” I exclaimed. J.P. nodded, “Exactly.” Driving scenes are staples. They’re a great way to deliver exposition, geographically move characters, and advance narrative all at once. I began to wrap my head around the Magicbox concept.

“Put any car inside Magicbox, and the LED screens project whatever street you want. Brian works with companies that have vast libraries of usable backgrounds. From Timbuktu to Morocco to New York City or Sunset Blvd., they provide the necessary plates. Also, they have vehicles rigged with cameras, as well as drones that can shoot custom plates for clients. Or, if a production wants to use their own backgrounds, Magicbox gives exact specifications for them to shoot their own plates.” I sat back on the sofa and envisioned shooting an Indiana Jones–style driving scene on the streets of Timbuktu, but in Burbank, and inside Magicbox.

That really made me think. Being a fan of The Mandalorian, I had a notion of what Brian was up to with Magicbox. I asked J.P., “Is Magicbox technology akin to how The Mandalorian was shot? Like the LED projections they used to render the backgrounds in that show?” In his gracious manner, J.P. said, “It’s better if Brian explains. It’s his company, his vision.”

After a while, Brian emerged from the Magicbox with his attendees. A quick Q&A was followed by information exchanges, handshakes, and smiles. When business concluded, Brian greeted me.

“Brian, what’s going on here?” I puzzled. He replied, “The short version is that we build these semi-trailers that look like that when they’re on the road (he pointed at the folded up Magicbox), and when we get to wherever we’re going, which is anywhere across the country and into Mexico and Canada, we drop the trailer and it takes about 35–45 minutes to transform into this.” Brian gestured toward the unfolded Magicbox trailer.

“You haul these all across North America?” I asked. J.P. interjected, “Brian recently went to the University of Pittsburgh to work with their athletic department, shooting their media content. He is going to Arizona soon to work with the San Francisco Giants at Spring Training.” Brian’s vision was becoming clear. Magicbox is like a Swiss Army Knife—mobile, versatile, and with numerous applications.

Brian led me in for show-and-tell. Inside the Magicbox, a Land Rover was parked on a huge hydraulic turntable, surrounded by state-of-the-art LED screens, with one hanging above the car. The screens displayed synced-up, moving visuals of a familiar local, tree-lined street that could be viewed from every angle. I recognized the street. I’ve driven it a hundred times. I felt my intuition was on point, so I got bold.

“So, this is what they did for The Mandalorian, but instead of suburban Studio City, they projected a weird planet in the background, right?” I asked. “Yeah, it’s called LED Volume, which is something that ILM developed back in 2018 for that show,” he informed. “Magicbox is a mobile version of the same technology that we developed. I saw what they did and I wanted to use it for my productions, but it cost tens of millions of dollars to build one and house it permanently on a soundstage. And, to rent that stage for one day, it was like $250K. I owned an ad agency and a production company in San Francisco at the time, so I decided to build one that I could afford to use.”

I asked, “Can you give me a watered-down, layman’s explanation?” He replied, “I’ll sum it up like this: Magicbox is a mobile LED studio that can go anywhere a production might be—a stage, a location, an event, wherever. It transforms quickly from a trailer into a soundstage with an LED Volume inside. You can get your shots in a controlled environment, move on, and it’s relatively low cost.” That’s pretty cool.

“Actors must like it, especially for the driving scenes, right?” I asked. “They love it. It’s convenient and safe. It also makes things easy for directors and cinematographers. No moving vehicles and instant resets,” Brian explained. I pondered, “So they can focus on performance and nuance without the hassle, and still capture a totally believable visual sequence?” Brian replied, “That’s the intent. We make it feel as real as possible for the actors. For example, we’ll rig a monitor inside the car so they can see where the turns in the road are and manipulate the steering wheel and react appropriately. It feels natural, it’s in a controlled environment, and they can focus on performance.”

I fixated on the Land Rover as the LED screens projected a realistic simulation of the Las Vegas Strip. I snapped out of it, but it felt very real. “Does anyone ever get motion sickness?” I asked. Brian laughed, “Maybe about one in fifty. It’s not a chronic issue at all, but it can happen.” I nodded and told him, “My compliments to the chef. Any physical reaction is a testament to you for delivering what you promise.” Brian gave a humble, yet confident shrug.

Magicbox’s mobility and cost efficiency have massive implications, especially for independent films working with minimal budgets. Filmmakers can utilize cutting-edge technology, not only to up production value, but to cut costs. Add in practical foreground sets and tangible props, and any location or environment is accessible through Magicbox technology. Indie filmmakers are free to go big and not limit their vision due to budget constraints. Think of it like an early Robert Rodriguez film on steroids.

Brian showed me a scene done for a production that takes place on the Catalina Island pier. “It would have been a nightmare and cost a fortune to send an entire crew there. We sent two guys out in the morning to shoot plates, they brought them back that afternoon, we put them up on the walls, and shot the scene. We built a physical portion of wood railing that was matched up with the screen and had a fan blowing,” Brian explained. They executed all of that on the same day.

Shooting with two cameras, at six different angles, and doing three full reads of a 5-page script at each angle, they set up, shot, and struck in 2 hours and 45 minutes, capturing 190 minutes of coverage. I watched the scene, and I gotta say, the raw composite footage looked fantastic.

“Why hasn’t this caught on like wildfire?” I asked. “Hopefully it will,” Brian replied. Well, he’s certainly pounding the pavement hosting demonstrations each week. “We’re doing a lot of TV right now—High Potential, 9-1-1, Grey’s Anatomy, Matlock... and commercials, sports, and other things. We want to work more with independent films too. I provide this technology to empower smaller productions and also service the big ones.”

Another unique Magicbox detail is that Brian vertically integrated user-friendly software which can be manipulated and reconfigured to meet the needs of each department. And, depending on the complexity, turnaround can be as quick as a request made before lunch and the adjustment ready by the time the A.D. yells, “We’re back!”

With sincere consideration to the state of things, Brian said, “We’re not here to take jobs. We want to enable everyone on set to work faster and more efficiently. We want to help create more productions.” You have to rally around that.

Brian Nowac’s Magicbox is truly innovative and can revolutionize the way movies and television are produced and shot. Magicbox is a supplement, not a replacement, and it has a multitude of applications. For instance, the folded up Magicbox trailer beside the LED studio trailer has a different design and intended use. When it’s unpacked, it’s a ready-to-go set piece for concerts, seminars, live TV broadcasts. Magicbox provides endless possibilities.

Brian created an amazing tool, and made it accessible to productions big and small. The high-end quality, diverse capability, and mobile convenience of Magicbox allows productions and filmmakers to dream big and let their imaginations run wild. Inspiring, innovative, and definitely empowering.

www.magicbox.ninja

title: The New Toluca Lake Farmers Market
author: James T. Bartlett
excerpt: Toluca Lake’s Farmers Market is Back and Better Than Ever

blocks.content:
Walking or driving alongside Riverside on a Sunday, you might have noticed that the Toluca Lake Farmers Market is back in a new location, with new management, new vendors, a desire to meet the community – and puppies and cookies!

Formerly located behind the Wells Fargo, it’s now down Talofa Avenue alongside the Red Maple, and as well as the expected fresh fruit, vegetables and flowers, there is a rotating variety of vendors offering everything from coffee, cookies and crystals to hummus, juice, paintings, pizzas and tamales.

Wandering around with a smile this recent Sunday was Amber Adame, the mastermind behind this new farmers market, and she sat down with The Tolucan Times to talk about Gaia Society, her non-profit that runs and organizes the market, and why she decided to come back to Toluca Lake.

“I used to vend here years ago – crystals, incense, jewelry – at the other market, and when that closed, I tried other markets as far as Santa Barbara all the way down to like San Diego, but it just wasn’t the same. Some you can kind of just tell it’s like for a money grab, and they don’t really care.”

More than that, she says that she really missed being within the community.

“Toluca Lake is very loving and welcoming, and so I was like: You know what? They’ve been waiting for their farmers market to come back, and I had been thinking about putting one on, and so this is the perfect place.”

Amber, 28, continues to run her Esoteric Beings stall here with help from her family, and feels that because she was a vendor and had been to lots of markets, she knew what was needed – though she admits the process was stressful.

“It’s a lot,” she laughs, “But I think the most important thing is that I like staying organized and staying on top of things. The Market is first-come-first-served, no contracts, which means it will be different every week, and there will always be something new. Like finding an Easter egg.”

“Already the word-of-mouth has been good,” she adds. “More vendors want to come.”

Amber graduated from Mount St. Mary’s University and was set to work in real estate and the mortgage industry, but that “kind of disappeared in the midst of COVID and everything, and I realized that I didn’t want to spend my whole life doing that. So, I kind of went back to like: okay, well what makes me happy? I missed the other vendors – we kind of became a family.”

One of the vendors she takes us to is David Brown – better known as Dapper Dave – who sells desserts and baked goods at the market. He invites people to play him at scissors, rock, paper, with your choice of cookie the prize if you win.

Brown, 30, lives on the border of Toluca Lake and Studio City, and explains that his nickname came about when he performed in Pittsburgh.

“I dressed nice, and the name kinda stuck,” he says, “But this all started after I moved from Pittsburgh to LA for music, and the pandemic hit. I always loved cooking so I started making food for some of my music industry connections. It did well, and so I took it more seriously, worked in some kitchens, went to school and started a business. Eventually one of my clients recommended I started going to farmers markets, and here I am.”

He has been baking professionally for six years now, and notes that in 2022 he went to the Amaury Guichon Pastry Academy in Nevada. His treats use organic ingredients and come in vegan/gluten free options.

And while his favorite cookie is the vanilla bean sugar, the most popular is chocolate chunk followed by “kitchen sink,” which has peanut butter cups, pretzels, and salted caramel.

Another young entrepreneur at what she calls this “hidden gem” farmers market is Chelsie Lynn from Woodland Hills.

Her company Unbare began as “almost a hobby,” she says, but after three months of heavy research and development, she began to sell at local events and farmers markets, initially selling handcrafted soaps and candles.

With more than an eye on the advantages of TikTok, she began formulating products from scratch, “and I rely heavily on real time feedback from customers to fine tune my formulations, making soaps, candles, body butters, lotions, and balms.”

This led to her once-a-month “drops” of new, limited-edition products which, she admits happily, “always sell out fast,” and are, of course, sustainable, cruelty-free, ethically sourced, and free from any harsh ingredients.

As for Amber, she was almost starting from scratch when she began looking into setting up the non-profit.

“So, I actually went to the library,” she says. “I lived closer to Northridge, so I was in their library, and I started reading and reading. By the time I had finished, this felt more like a passion project to me.”

Farmers markets are meant to be non-profit or run by government agencies, and Amber says that once she understood how Burbank’s bureaucracy works, the city officials were extremely helpful.

She initially reached out to a couple of vendors she already knew, though the actual fruit and vegetable vendors were tougher to get, as many travel far longer distances and often visit several markets on different weekends.

Now walking around the market, Amber explains that she and her handful of part-time employees arrive at the site around 6:30am and do a trash clean-up, then make sure that vendors know exactly where they’re located, and help them load in – most are ready to go.

“Talofa Ave is a small space, so we tell the vendors on one side of the street to come in via the alleyway, and the others to turn in from Riverside – so there are no jams.”

They also make sure vendors don’t park in the areas belonging to the adjacent businesses, set up a dining table, and will check in with vendors throughout the day – even handing out bottles of water.

“But we’re here rain or shine,” says Amber. They then do a second trash clean-up before leaving.

The response from customers and vendors alike has been very positive she says, and that she plans to invite musicians and bands too, though she assures us that the music will not be blasting everywhere.

Activities for kids are on her radar as well, and Pawsitive Beginnings puppy adoptions will be back soon too.

“It was extremely popular, and so far, we’ve managed to get like some 60 puppies adopted into the community. We’d love an ice cream vendor in the summer too,” she laughs.

Toluca Farmers Market is open every Sunday from 9am

@tolucalakefarmersmarket
www.gaiasociety.org
